title: Mixed Venous Saturations: SvO2 and ScvO2

abridged.content:
Mixed venous saturation - the global oxygen balance signal

SvO₂ is the oxygen saturation of pulmonary artery blood after systemic venous blood has mixed. It reflects the balance between oxygen delivery and oxygen consumption. A usual adult range is 60-80%, with many CTICU targets around ≥65-70% if the rest of the picture agrees.

ScvO₂ is central venous oxygen saturation from the SVC/right atrial region. It is not identical to SvO₂ because it does not fully include lower body and coronary sinus contributions. Trends can help, but direct equivalence is unsafe in shock.

Low SvO₂ usually means increased extraction because delivery is low, demand is high, or both. Causes include low CO, anaemia, hypoxaemia, fever, shivering, pain, agitation, sepsis, seizures, and increased work of breathing.

High SvO₂ is not automatically reassuring. It may mean high delivery and low demand, but it may also indicate impaired extraction, microcirculatory shunting, sepsis, hypothermia, deep sedation, mitochondrial dysfunction, or sampling error.

expanded.content:
Mixed venous saturation - the global oxygen balance signal

This topic must be understood as a mechanism, not as a list of numbers. In CTICU, the same numerical value can mean different things depending on rhythm, ventricular function, vascular tone, ventilation, oxygen content, temperature, bleeding, surgical context, and the patient’s response over time.

SvO2 physiology

SvO2 physiology is a core component of this topic. It must be interpreted through the CTICU pattern rather than as an isolated number. The clinical meaning depends on pressure, flow, filling, oxygen delivery, oxygen demand, waveform quality, and surgical context.

Mechanistically, SvO2 physiology affects the relationship between ventricular filling, pressure generation, ejection, venous return, and tissue oxygen delivery. When this variable changes, the arterial waveform, pulse pressure, cardiac index, filling pressures, lactate, urine output, and venous saturation may change before the patient looks obviously unstable.

At the bedside, assess SvO2 physiology by looking for concordance. A trustworthy interpretation requires the monitor value, waveform, clinical perfusion, recent trend, and response to therapy to point in the same direction. If the number and patient disagree, validate the equipment and escalate the physiological question rather than treating the number alone.

When this variable changes, the arterial waveform, pulse pressure, cardiac index, filling pressures, lactate, urine output, and venous saturation may change before the patient looks obviously unstable.

At the bedside, assess bedside algorithm by looking for concordance. A trustworthy interpretation requires the monitor value, waveform, clinical perfusion, recent trend, and response to therapy to point in the same direction. If the number and patient disagree, validate the equipment and escalate the physiological question rather than treating the number alone.

CTICU bedside integration

Start by confirming the measurement is real: level, zero, waveform quality, sampling site, timing, rhythm, and patient position. Then separate pressure from flow. Ask whether the patient has adequate cardiac output, adequate vascular tone, acceptable filling pressures, tolerable afterload, adequate oxygen content, and acceptable oxygen demand.

Next, decide which mechanism is most likely failing: preload, afterload, contractility, rhythm, obstruction, oxygen content, or oxygen demand. The intervention follows the mechanism. Fluid is for preload-responsive and fluid-tolerant physiology. Vasopressor is for inadequate vascular tone or perfusion pressure. Inotropy is for inadequate pump force. Ventilation and oxygenation changes are haemodynamic interventions when PVR or work of breathing is part of the problem.

Finally, reassess after every intervention. A useful intervention improves flow or oxygen economy: stronger pulse pressure, improved CI, better urine output, falling lactate, improved SvO₂, improved skin perfusion, or reduced vasoactive requirement. A harmful intervention raises filling pressure, worsens oxygenation, worsens RV dilation, increases afterload excessively, or improves MAP while lactate and SvO₂ worsen.

Pattern table

FindingMeaningCTICU action
Rising lactateOxygen delivery-demand mismatch or metabolic stressCheck CO/CI, Hb, SaO₂, temperature, vasoactive dose, and perfusion
Low urine outputLow renal perfusion, venous congestion, AKI, or vasoconstrictionAssess MAP, CI, CVP, fluid balance, renal trend, and congestion
Falling SvO₂Increased extraction from low delivery or high demandAssess flow, Hb, oxygenation, fever, shivering, pain, and WOB

pearls.content:
Clinical Pearls - Mixed Venous Saturations: SvO2 and ScvO2

Pearl 1 - Treat the mechanism, not the number

Mixed Venous Saturations: SvO2 and ScvO2 becomes clinically useful only when linked to preload, afterload, contractility, rhythm, oxygen delivery, and demand.

Pearl 2 - Trends beat snapshots

A changing trend with worsening lactate, urine output, pulse pressure, or SvO₂ is more important than a single apparently acceptable value.

Pearl 3 - Validate before acting

Check waveform, zero, level, sample site, timing, rhythm, ventilation, and patient position before escalating therapy based on a number.

Pearl 4 - The RV and LV may need different things

LV-focused management can harm the RV if PVR, CVP, and septal shift are ignored.

equipment.content:
Equipment Tie-In - Mixed Venous Saturations: SvO2 and ScvO2

Arterial line: shows pressure, pulse pressure, upstroke, damping, and response to therapy.

CVP/PAC: helps separate filling pressure, PA pressure, CO/CI, SVR, PVR, and SvO₂ when present.

Echo: identifies chamber size, LV/RV function, septal shift, tamponade, valve problems, and gross filling state.

Ventilator: affects venous return, PVR, oxygenation, PaCO₂, pH, and work of breathing.

ABG/POCT: provides lactate, pH, PaCO₂, PaO₂, Hb, potassium, ionised calcium, and other variables that change haemodynamics.

targets.content:
Targets and Numbers - Mixed Venous Saturations: SvO2 and ScvO2

Targets are pattern-based rather than isolated. Common CTICU reference points include MAP 65-85 mmHg, CI ≥2.2 L/min/m², urine output ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr, lactate <2 mmol/L or falling, SvO₂ 60-80% or ≥65-70% when appropriate, CVP often 5-12 mmHg depending context, and PAWP often 8-15 mmHg if valid. Patient type, surgical procedure, RV risk, bleeding risk, valve repair, aortic surgery, and chronic hypertension may shift targets.

tables.heading: Core values and interpretation
rows: Must be matched with CI, lactate, UO, and SvO₂
Low with poor perfusion suggests low-output physiology
High with shock suggests RV/obstructive/congestive physiology
High with hypoxia suggests congestion or LV dysfunction
Rising suggests delivery-demand mismatch or severe stress
Low in low flow, venous congestion, AKI, or vasoconstriction

tables.heading: CTICU pattern recognition
rows: Warm, bounding pulse, low diastolic pressure
Narrow pulse pressure, cool, low UO

quizQuestions.question: What is the safest way to interpret Mixed Venous Saturations: SvO2 and ScvO2 in CTICU?
options: As part of a full haemodynamic pattern
By treating every abnormal value with fluid
quizQuestions.explanation: Mixed Venous Saturations: SvO2 and ScvO2 must be interpreted with pressure, flow, waveform quality, rhythm, oxygen delivery, demand, and clinical response.

quizQuestions.question: A patient has conflicting data related to Mixed Venous Saturations: SvO2 and ScvO2. What should happen first?
options: Validate the measurement and correlate with the clinical picture
Assume the number is correct and treat immediately
Give a large fluid bolus automatically
quizQuestions.explanation: Measurement validity and clinical correlation come first because transducer error, sampling error, damping, rhythm, ventilation, and patient position can all change interpretation.

quizQuestions.question: Why is isolated interpretation of Mixed Venous Saturations: SvO2 and ScvO2 dangerous after cardiac surgery?
options: Because preload, afterload, contractility, rhythm, ventilation, oxygen content, and demand interact
Because CTICU patients do not need haemodynamic monitoring
Because normal MAP always proves normal flow
Because all post-op cardiac patients have identical physiology
quizQuestions.explanation: After cardiac surgery the haemodynamic system is interactive. A single value can reflect multiple mechanisms and can be misleading unless interpreted with the full CTICU pattern.
